# Federal energy backdrop reference — US EIA + LBNL/DOE + IRA statute

Committed federal backdrop for the campus load: the federal energy **policy levers**
that bear on a load/generation of this profile, and the US national energy **output /
statistics** the facility's demand is read against. The top of the grid & regulatory
stack (issue #98, epic #93). It is the macro backdrop the consumer-cost thread (issue
#91) reads against. Regenerated by `bosc federal`; the site/scenario read
`federal-energy.yaml` (no live pull).

## Files

| File | What | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `federal-energy.yaml` | The federal clean-energy **policy levers** (IRA section 45/48 + tech-neutral 45Y/48E, section 45V/45X, DOE LPO, DOE grid resilience), each mapped to what it applies to and its direction on cost; the US national **output/statistics** (total net generation, data-center share of US load now + 2028 projection, average retail price); and the **campus-vs-national** integration (campus consumption as a share of US data-center load and US total generation). Regenerated by `bosc federal`. (Issue #98, epic #93.) | Policy levers: **Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (Pub. L. 117-169)** / IRS guidance + DOE LPO / IIJA section 40101. Output: **EIA** national (Electric Power Annual, retail price) + **LBNL/DOE 2024 United States Data Center Energy Usage Report**. Both `source: reference`, transcribed. Campus shares `source: derived` from `facility_draw` (#87). |

## The campus vs the federal backdrop

`bosc.grid.policy.derive_federal_backdrop` links the facility's first-class total
**`facility_draw`** (`bosc.facility.power`, issue #87 — IT load x PUE, ~348 MW central)
to the national figures:

- **Annual consumption** = facility draw x 8760 h x load factor (~0.9, assumption) —
  ~2,743 GWh/yr, the same convention as #91/#94/#95.
- **Share of US data-center load** = that consumption / (US data-center use ~176 TWh
  x 1000) — ~1.6%.
- **Share of US total net generation** = consumption / (~4,430 TWh x 1000) — ~0.06%.

The policy levers map each credit/program to its **direction on cost** (`lowers
clean-gen cost` / `tangential` / `n/a`) — a direction, never a quantified offset. The
clean-generation credits (section 45 PTC / section 48 ITC and their tech-neutral
successors 45Y/48E, plus DOE LPO loan guarantees) **lower the cost of clean generation
the campus could procure / PPA**; section 45V (clean hydrogen) and section 45X
(advanced manufacturing) are **tangential**; DOE grid resilience bears on
deliverability, not a direct campus credit.

## Known gaps & caveats

- **EIA national output is a live connector pull (#98/#120).** US net generation
  (`ELEC.GEN.ALL-US-99.A`) and US average retail price (`ELEC.PRICE.US-ALL.A`) are
  pulled from the EIA seriesid connector (`source: connector`, keyed via
  `BOSC_EIA_API_KEY`; offline replays `tests/fixtures/economics/eia/`). The
  **data-center share** (~176 TWh / ~4.4% / ~9.5% by 2028) stays a **report-cited
  reference** (LBNL/DOE 2024) — it is not on the EIA API.
- **Figures are as-of the latest published vintage.** US net generation ~**4,430 TWh/yr**
  (EIA `ELEC.GEN.ALL-US-99.A`, 2025); US average retail price ~**13.63 cents/kWh**, all
  sectors (EIA `ELEC.PRICE.US-ALL.A`, 2025); US data-center use ~176 TWh in 2023 (~4.4%
  of US total), projected to ~6.7-12% of US load by 2028 (LBNL/DOE 2024). The 2028
  projection stored is the ~9.5% midpoint of the published ~6.7-12% range.
- **Policy applicability is profile-level, not a facility claim.** Which levers apply
  depends on the campus's **undisclosed** generation / procurement choices; the levers
  are mapped to a load/generation of this profile, not asserted as claimed by this
  facility. **Nothing here is a facility disclosure.**
- **Cost direction is direction-of-cost only**, not a quantified offset — the dollar
  value depends on undisclosed contracting.
- **National projections are scenario-dependent**; transcribed figures must be verified
  against the cited primary sources before use in any quantitative claim. Never
  fabricate; prefer omission over invention.
